hub_subtitle: Business English para seguros, reuniões e entrevistas — e o desbloqueio da fala, do primeiro dia ao último

lesson.menu_title: Can You Say That Again? -- Clarification and Survival Strategies
lesson.menu_desc: A aula em que não entender deixa de ser o fim da conversa. Ela trava sempre no mesmo ponto: o outro fala rápido, uma palavra passa voando, e ela congela — e por dentro pensa “pronto, ele descobriu que meu inglês é ruim”. Aqui está o segredo que muda tudo: o americano também não entende metade das vezes — e ele simplesmente pergunta. "Sorry, could you repeat that?" não é frase de iniciante: é a frase que o diretor de Miami mais usa. Esta aula não tem gramática nova — ela pega o could da aula 9 e as perguntas da aula 8 e monta um kit de sobrevivência de seis palavras (repeat, understand, mean, catch, again, moment) e cinco frases que mantêm qualquer ligação viva. A virada: no seguro, pedir para repetir não é fraqueza — quem adivinha um número perde a conta; quem pergunta, não. -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 12 -- Can You Say That Again?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Paula Morales | Lesson 12 -- Can You Say That Again?
lesson.grammar_point: clarification and repair strategies -- could you repeat/say that again? for the whole sentence, could you speak more slowly? for the speed, what does [word] mean? for one word, and just a moment / I didn't catch that to stay in the conversation

listenings.text: Hi, Paula. David here. You have a call with Greg this week, and I know exactly what you are afraid of. You are afraid he says something fast, you do not catch it, and he finds out your English is not perfect. So let me give you the one thing nobody gives you. Ready? He already knows your English is not perfect. He does not care. And here is the part you will not believe: I am American, I was born with this language, and I ask people to repeat things every single day. On the phone, in meetings, with my own mother. Sorry, could you say that again? What does that mean? One moment, let me check. These are not beginner phrases, Paula. These are the phrases everybody uses, in every language, all day. The person who does not understand and asks is fine. The person who does not understand and stays silent, and nods, and guesses the number wrong, that person is the problem. When you do not catch something on that call, do not freeze, and do not go back to Portuguese. Just ask. Could you repeat that? Four words. They keep the whole call alive, and nobody, nobody, thinks less of you for saying them. The only bad move is guessing. See you after the call.

Good morning, Paula. Sofia here. One thing before your call, and it is not about English. I have watched this company for twenty years, and I want to tell you who wins here. It is not the person with the best English. It is the person who asks. When a broker gives me a word I do not know, I stop him. What does that mean? When a client says a number too fast, I say it back, and I ask him to repeat. Every time. The people who ask are the professionals, Paula. The ones who nod and smile and understood nothing, those are the ones I worry about, because in our business a wrong word is not an embarrassment, it is somebody's factory, it is money. So when you are on that call with Greg and something goes past you, do exactly what I do. Say, sorry, could you repeat that? Ask what the word means. Take a moment. Nobody at this company has ever lost an account for asking a question. Not one. People lose accounts for guessing, for nodding, for being too proud to say I did not catch that. You are not going into that call to be perfect. You are going in to understand. And understanding starts with asking. See you Monday.

reading.rtitle: The Survival Kit — Zurich Brazil, Note for International Calls
paras: Every week, someone at Zurich Brazil is on a call in English, and the other person says a number, a name, or a word too fast. This note is here to say one thing, and it is the whole note: when you do not catch it, you ask. You do not guess. A wrong number in our business is not an embarrassment — it is money.

How you ask. There are four lines, and you already have them. "Sorry, could you repeat that?" — for the whole sentence. "Could you speak more slowly?" — when you have the words but not the speed. "What does that word mean?" — when it is only one word. And "Just a moment" — when you need three seconds to find the file or the number. That is the entire kit. Four lines, and no call can beat them.

Here is the part people do not believe. The Americans on the call do it too. Every week. Greg, the director in Miami, asks "sorry, say that again?" more than anyone on this floor. He is not a beginner — he is the director. Asking someone to repeat is not weakness, and it never was. In twenty countries and twenty accents, everybody misses words. The professional asks. The beginner nods and guesses.

Two people from this office. Ricardo, broker relations: "For a year I nodded on every call and understood half of it. Then I started to ask — could you repeat that? Nobody thought less of me. They slowed down, and my numbers stopped being wrong." And Sofia, commercial manager: "The people who ask are the professionals. The ones who guess are the ones I worry about. Nobody at this company ever lost an account for asking someone to repeat. People lose accounts for guessing."
reading.source: Zurich Brazil — internal note, international desk

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the note?
choices: The note explains how to write a report in English before a call.
The note is Zurich's clarification kit: when you don't catch something on a call, you ask — you don't guess — and asking to repeat is professional, not weak. Even the American director does it.
The note says only Americans are allowed to ask people to repeat.

items: When you do not catch something, the note says you should guess the number.
The opposite, and it is the whole note: "when you do not catch it, you ask. You do not guess." It even says why — "a wrong number in our business is not an embarrassment, it is money."
The note gives you four lines you can use to ask.
Yes: "Sorry, could you repeat that?", "Could you speak more slowly?", "What does that word mean?", and "Just a moment." The note calls it "the entire kit".
The note says only beginners ask people to repeat.
The opposite. "Greg, the director in Miami, asks 'sorry, say that again?' more than anyone on this floor. He is not a beginner — he is the director." Asking is not weakness.
Ricardo says people thought less of him when he started to ask.
He says the opposite: "Nobody thought less of me. They slowed down, and my numbers stopped being wrong."
Sofia says people lose accounts for asking someone to repeat.
The opposite: "Nobody at this company ever lost an account for asking someone to repeat. People lose accounts for guessing."

bank.label: When it goes past you / Quando passa voando
items: Could you say that again, please?

bank.label: One word, and buying time / Uma palavra — e ganhar tempo
items: Just a moment, let me check.

items.situation: You are on a video call. The other person said a long sentence, and all of it went past you. Ask for the whole thing again -- politely.
tips: Sorry -- could you say that again, please?

items.situation: You have the words, but the person is speaking very fast. Do not ask about the words -- ask only about the speed.
tips: Sorry -- could you speak more slowly, please?

items.situation: The client said one word you do not know: "deductible". Everything else was clear. Ask what that one word means.
tips: Sorry, what does that word mean?

items.situation: You need three seconds to find the number in your file. Do not go silent, and do not panic -- buy the time out loud.
tips: Just a moment, let me check.

items.situation: You did not catch a name on the phone. You have most of it, but not the spelling. Ask.
The line was bad and you missed the last part of what Greg said. Say you didn't catch it -- and do not say sorry three times.
tips: I didn't catch the last part. Could you repeat it?
